speaker: interviewer
text: Alex Stern, February 28, 1996, take five. Alex, you were talking about how important the future of Israel is for the Jewish religion to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about the way the Jewish people live in America when you got to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] first of all, I went to Cuba first. But I always-- America was always for me the champion of freedom and whatever America stands for. And the reason for me being alive was the American army, so I was always proud of [SIGHS] being in the of-- of America. But I was in Cuba, I was very satisfied there. But when Castro came in power, I had to leave. Because Communism was never one of my best [LAUGHS] ideals. And when I came to the United States in 1960, I found out that Cuba was going to become a communist country. And I [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] came to Dallas first. And then we went to-- to New York. My sister lived in Dallas. And my brother was in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I was in New York in 1973, I received a letter from the German consulate that I'm wanted on a trial, that they're gonna need me to testify for the commandant from Gunthergrube, Alois Frey and Willi Sawatzki. And I should go to Germany, to Frankfurt, and testify what I know about the camp and about both of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I was saying before, they were just plain murderers. They didn't-- Human life was no-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] their-- their best interest. They were killing people like they were flies. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think when you got this letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- to go back to Germany wasn't my-- my favorite thing. As a matter of fact, my wife wanted to back to Hungary. And I never felt like going. But to testify against murder like that, I felt it was my obligation. And it was something I had do. And I did go. So the trial was supposed to take two weeks. And I really-- [SIGHS] I-- I couldn't sleep. I couldn't eat. I was like-- like desperate. And the trial did end earlier, after 10 days. And they didn't need me no more. So I picked myself up, and I went to Spain. Because I couldn't stand Germany. And I don't think I could ever stand going to Germany again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the SS men that were on trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I did. Of course, they didn't look like with those-- those-- those black boots, and those-- those sharp uniforms, and those demanding voices, and those-- those, I would call them, toy soldiers, or whatever. They-- they looked broken down, like we were in the camps. But they were fed. And they were taken care of. But at least they-- they-- they caught them. And they took them to trial. I don't know how Germany is going to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] get their sentences, what they deserve. But I could-- I did the best I could. I-- I testified what I knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel that you were able to testify against these Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in a way, I felt satisfaction. But after that, I was kind-- completely-- my blood pressure went up. And [SIGHS] I was a different person. You see, my-- my hands always were a little bit shaky. But after the trial, I couldn't sign my name. I couldn't hold a-- a pen in my hand. So it did a lot of damage to my nervous system. And my blood pressure went up. And I wasn't the same person after that. But I still felt satisfaction that I-- I did my-- my part where I could to-- to show these murderers that's not the way to-- for civilized people to-- to-- to conduct an army, or a life or a country, or whatever they call it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] you know, it's mixed emotions. When I was sitting, I used to go to the trial with a-- They had a streetcar there. And I heard the German talking next to me. Sometimes I used to put my hands over my ears. That's how much it-- it bothered me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I went through it. And all day after the trial, I was just plain sitting in the hotel. I could never even go out in the street. I was just sitting, and waiting, to take end. And I went to Spain right away. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in general, it was a surprise to me that after so many years, they-- they got a hold on them. Because they were real criminals. I mean, when I say criminal, what they did in that camp, people died there or in any other camp. And they-- no matter what they say, they just did their duty. But killing people, I don't think you can consider a duty, no matter who it is or what reason it is. Just plain killing people, it was completely out of our reasoning or our way of justice. I hope they learned their lesson and the whole world learned their lesson, that fascism is not the way of life for nobody, and not for the Jews, and not for Americans, and not even for Germans. They did it and got away with it. But finally, we caught up with them. So justice survived. And I never-- I never stopped listening to the trials in Nuremberg, and in Budapest, and all of the countries where they went to trial. And of course, the-- the trial in-- in Israel, of-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the glass cage. They caught-- they caught up with them. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in Argentina, they got him to Israel, the only, really one, who they caught up with. And he was convicted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, well, it's not really revenge. To me, it's more to show the world, that no matter how-- you-- you can't consider that this was the right way to do it. And we still have skinheads, and fascism. And no matter what you call it, to me, it's still wrong.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's never going to bring out the good in people. It only brings the worst part of them. So that's my main [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] thinking, that it could never happen again for the well-being of not only my children, but everybody, whoever believes in justice and democracy, that they should live up to it. And I hope as far as I'm concerned, not much to change or do. But the kids, and the grandchildren, and the future generations, and that's what I'm so happy, that this is done. And that the Holocaust Memorial in-- in Washington, it's one of the biggest things that they ever did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since you've been an American, were you able to build up your business again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. I made a pretty good living here. Of course, I came from Cuba with a very good trade. I mean, I'm in the garment industry. I worked for a very good company. I worked for Geoffrey Beene. And I had good pay. They-- they how to say-- they saw my-- my-- my knowledge in the industry. And I did well. I did all right. After years, I always dreamed to save up money and build something of my own. From the first day I got here, I told my wife, from every pay day, you put something aside. I want to have my own factory and build something up for my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So thank God, no complaints. We did all right. And we always tried hard to-- and most of all, my wife always, always was fighting the same way. We did together everything. Thank God for that. Are you retired now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I am, [LAUGHS] a proud retired grandfather. Not a great-grandfather yet, but I hope if I live long enough, I'll have one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alex, can you tell me, have your religious beliefs changed since before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: It really didn't change. It depends how you look at it. My religious feelings are just the same. There's only one God, and no matter-- the 10 Commandments. I always told my children. If you follow the 10 Commandments, you cannot be a bad person. No matter what your religion is, or whom you believe in. 10 Commandments is a way of life. And if you know that you're not supposed to kill, there's not going to be another Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my religious beliefs did not change at all. On the contrary, I believe that people have to have a religion, because whoever is a killer, cannot have religion. Because the religions say, you should not kill.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's exactly why it'll never-- it'll never be. I am not wearing a beard. Or, I'm not fanatic. But I'm just as religious as any next guy, whenever-- whoever feels himself that he's religious. And I'm very proud of it. And I hope my kids will be, as much as they're able to. And they all had their bar mitzvahs. And they all are good children. And we used to make our Seder every year together. And they know everything and Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur. They used to go to shul with me. Now, I'm retired. I still [INAUDIBLE] more in Florida. But they know their-- they know their religion. And I hope they stick to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Sure. I am hoping, I am hoping that after all us, you get other people to tell their part of their story. And that's the only way that you can show the world that this existed. And it's not that we made it up. Or, whoever says that it was a Jewish story or a Jewish-- they wanted to get their own country. We did get our own country. But not because we made it up. We fought for it, and we died for it. And we're-- we're willing to keep on dying for it. It's our country. It was our country 2,000 years ago. And it was a country of our ancestors. We-- we stick to it. It is ours. I went there every year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Thank you. Alex, could you introduce your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my lovely wife, Julia, for 50 years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I met her in Budapest at my aunt's house. And we've been happy together since. And I have to thank her for a lot of things. She's very, very effective and very loving. She loves her whole family. She lives for her family. And I hope my kids learn about this, that you have to live for your family. You have to do whatever you can for your family. It's very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Julia, would you like to say something to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We are 50 years married and 50 wonderful years. And he is a wonderful father, and a wonderful husband. And he took care of me all along, all these years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Should he say his number while your photographing him?

speaker: interviewer
text: He did say his number.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think he-- you did your number, didn't you? Oh, wait a minute. Did you say your number? What is your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: My number is 89462.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alex, when did you get this hole in you back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we were leaving Flossenburg, and we went on the death march. The German SS man told us to run down the-- on the side of the highway if you want to live. And once we were in the middle of the trees, they started shooting. And they shot me in the middle of my back and on my shoulder. And I was conscious, probably, very soon after that. Because I didn't know what happened since then. That's when I got my wound there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the bullet removed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, the bullet was not removed. According to X-rays, there was shrapnel there. And I-- for-- for-- for years, it caused me pain. In Cuba, they said that it dissolved, or it at least stopped. It wasn't bothering me no more. But since then, about two or three years, I had a lot of pains from it. But then it stopped. And thank God, since then, I am-- it didn't bother me no more. This is my father, Simon, born in 1896. And this is my mother, Cecelia, born in 1897. My mother was born in Sighet. And my father was born in Csolnok, Hungary. And my mother was born in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sighet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Early '30s.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my Aunt Regina and my cousins. She was born in Sighet, my mother's sister. And this was taken in 1930, in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Simon Stern. The picture was taken in Romania, in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in the war, in 1944. And that little guy on the right corner, that's me. We were in a-- in a working camp-- situation there on the Hungarian regime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, Iztak, taken in Romania, during the war, in 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Hungary. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken after the war in Romania. That's my uncle. And the one, second on the left, that's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the wedding of my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: David Stern. This our wedding picture, in 1946, in Germany, my wife and myself. It was taken in Cham.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are the names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Julia and Alex Stern. This is a picture taken by the Dallas newspaper, when we came from Cuba, in 1960. It's my-- my wife, Julia, and my son, Abraham, and Margaret, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dallas. And this is the letter I got from consule from Germany, to go to Frankfurt for the trial of Alois Frey and Willi Savatski, to testify against them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1973. This is my grandsons, David Hecht and Joshua Hecht. They live in Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, 1945. No, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my granddaughter, Jackie, and myself, Alex Stern, taken on the occasion of my-- of the wedding of my daughter, Margaret, in 1982.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alex, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my granddaughter, Melissa and Staci. And that's Julia and Alex Stern, taken on the occasion of my son Abe's wedding, in New York, in 1995.
